metadata.description: Chinese vocabulary for shoes, sizes, styles, and payment methods

content.name: measure_word_pair
content.side_e: wǒ xiǎng mǎi yì shuāng xīn xié.
content.side_f: I want to buy a pair of new shoes.
content.side_g: 双 is the measure word for items that come in natural pairs (shoes 鞋, chopsticks 筷子, eyes 眼睛, hands 手). Contrast with 对 (duì), which pairs items by convention rather than nature (earrings, couples). Always use 一双 before 鞋, never 一个鞋.

content.name: shoes
content.side_e: zhè shuāng xié dàxiǎo hěn héshì.
content.side_f: This pair of shoes fits well in size.
content.side_g: 鞋 is a generic noun for shoes/footwear; in speech it's usually 鞋子 (xiézi) in mainland northern usage, but 鞋 alone is fine in shop contexts. Common collocations: 穿鞋 (put on shoes), 一双鞋, 皮鞋 (leather shoes), 运动鞋 (sneakers).

content.name: exchange
content.side_e: zhè shuāng xié tài xiǎo, néng bu néng huàn yì shuāng dà yìdiǎnr de?
content.side_f: These shoes are too small — can you exchange them for a bigger pair?
content.side_g: 换 covers both 'exchange one for another' (换一件, 换钱) and 'change/switch' (换车 change trains, 换工作 change jobs). In shopping, 给我换一X ba is the standard polite request. Do not confuse with 还 (huán, to return).

content.name: same_alike
content.side_e: zhè jiàn chènshān hé nà jiàn yíyàng.
content.side_f: This shirt is the same as that one.
content.side_g: 一样 functions as a stative adjective meaning 'the same/alike'. Pattern: A 跟/和 B 一样 (+ adjective) — e.g., 他跟我一样高. Negation is A 跟 B 不一样. Do not say 很一样; degree is already built in.

content.name: although
content.side_e: suīrán huā le bù shǎo qián, kěshì mǎi de dōngxi dōu hěn hǎo.
content.side_f: Although we spent quite a bit, everything we bought is great.
content.side_g: 虽然 almost always pairs with 可是/但是/不过 in the second clause ('although…, still…'). It can appear before or after the subject: 虽然他很忙 / 他虽然很忙. Don't drop the second-clause conjunction in writing, even though English 'although' stands alone.

content.name: size
content.side_e: zhè tiáo kùzi dàxiǎo hěn héshì, chángduǎn yě gānghǎo.
content.side_f: These pants fit well in size, and the length is just right.
content.side_g: 大小 is an antonym-compound noun (lit. 'big-small') meaning 'size,' parallel to 长短 (length) and 多少 (quantity). It's the generic noun for size; 号 (hào) is the specific numeric size. Do not say 你的大小是多少 — use 你穿多大号?

content.name: brown_coffee_color
content.side_e: wǒ xiǎng kànkan kāfēisè de xié.
content.side_f: I'd like to see the brown shoes.
content.side_g: 咖啡色 literally means 'coffee color' and is the most common word for brown in everyday speech; 棕色 (zōngsè) is more formal/written. The 色 suffix attaches to many color nouns (红色, 黑色, 蓝色) when used as standalone nominals.

content.name: measure_word_kinds
content.side_e: zhè zhǒng xié zhǐ yǒu hēisè de.
content.side_f: This kind of shoe only comes in black.
content.side_g: 种 classifies types/kinds/varieties, not individual items: 这种鞋 (this kind of shoe), 一种颜色 (one kind of color), 两种方法 (two methods). Contrast with 双 (natural pairs) and 件 (articles of clothing). 种 also appears in 种类 (category).

content.name: black
content.side_e: hēisè de kùzi pèi lánsè de chènshān hěn hǎokàn.
content.side_f: Black pants with a blue shirt look great (lit. match well).
content.side_g: 黑 is the monosyllabic root; as a standalone color noun use 黑色. 黑 compounds widely: 黑板 (blackboard), 黑夜 (dark night), 黑色幽默 (black humor). As an adjective before a noun, both 黑裤子 and 黑色的裤子 are acceptable, the latter slightly more formal.

content.name: style
content.side_e: zhè jiàn yàngzi tǐng hǎo de.
content.side_f: This one's style is pretty nice.
content.side_g: 样子 means 'style/appearance/look' of a physical item or a person (他的样子很累 'he looks tired'). For abstract 'style/genre' use 风格 (fēnggé). Common: 样子好看, 什么样子, 老样子 (same as always). Contrast with 种类 (category) and 款式 (formal 'model/design').

content.name: quite_rather
content.side_e: zhè shuāng xié tǐng hǎokàn de.
content.side_f: These shoes are quite nice-looking.
content.side_g: 挺 is a colloquial degree adverb roughly equivalent to 'pretty/quite,' softer than 很 and warmer than 比较. It typically takes the frame 挺 + adj + 的 (挺好的, 挺忙的); dropping 的 sounds truncated. Common in northern spoken Mandarin; 蛮 (mán) is the southern equivalent.

content.name: it
content.side_e: zhè jiàn chènshān hěn hǎokàn, wǒ jiù yào tā le.
content.side_f: This shirt looks great — I'll take it.
content.side_g: 它 is the neuter third-person pronoun for things and (non-pet) animals. It is far less used than English 'it': Chinese frequently drops the object entirely when context is clear. Pronounced identically to 他/她 (tā); the distinction is purely written. Plural is 它们.

content.name: here
content.side_e: shìyījiān zài zhèr ma?
content.side_f: Is the fitting room here?
content.side_g: 这儿 is the northern/Beijing colloquial form of 'here,' equivalent to 这里 (zhèlǐ), which is more neutral/southern/written. Use 在这儿 (here), 到这儿来 (come here), 从这儿 (from here). The 儿 suffix (erhua) is obligatory in pronunciation, not a separate syllable.

content.name: pay_with_card
content.side_a: to pay with a credit card
content.side_e: wǒ kěyǐ shuā kǎ ma?
content.side_f: Can I pay by card? (lit. swipe card)
content.side_g: 刷卡 is a verb-object compound: 刷 (swipe) + 卡 (card). It's separable — 刷了卡, 刷一下卡, 刷过卡. The umbrella term covers credit/debit cards. In modern China, 扫码 (sǎo mǎ, scan QR code) has largely replaced 刷卡; 刷卡 still works in retail and formal contexts.

content.name: brush_swipe
content.side_e: qǐng nín shuā yíxià kǎ.
content.side_f: Please swipe your card.
content.side_g: 刷 originally means 'to brush/scrub' (刷牙 brush teeth, 刷鞋 brush shoes) and by extension 'to swipe through a reader' (刷卡, 刷门禁). Internet slang extends it further: 刷微博 (scroll Weibo), 刷剧 (binge-watch), 刷屏 (flood a feed). Context disambiguates.

content.name: card
content.side_e: nín yǒu kǎ ma?
content.side_f: Do you have a card?
content.side_g: 卡 is a transliteration of English 'card' and functions as a free morpheme: 信用卡, 银行卡, 饭卡, 公交卡, 贺卡 (greeting card). As a full verb 卡 (qiǎ) means 'to get stuck' — a different reading. In payment contexts always kǎ.

content.name: receive_accept
content.side_e: duìbuqǐ, wǒmen bù shōu xìnyòngkǎ.
content.side_f: Sorry, we don't accept credit cards.
content.side_g: 收 means 'to accept/take in/collect' (收钱, 收信 receive a letter, 收礼物 accept a gift). In commerce 我们不收…/我们只收… is the standard formula for payment acceptance. Related compounds: 收到 (have received), 收据 (receipt), 收入 (income). Contrast with 接 (jiē, meet/receive a person) and 得到 (dédào, obtain).

content.name: credit_card
content.side_e: zhèr shōu xìnyòngkǎ ma?
content.side_f: Do you accept credit cards here?
content.side_g: 信用卡 literally 'credit card' (信用 trust/credit + 卡 card). In mainland usage, 信用卡 specifically implies a revolving-credit card; debit cards are 储蓄卡 (chǔxùkǎ) or 借记卡. Questions: 收不收信用卡? / 能刷信用卡吗? / 可以用信用卡付吗?

content.name: however_but
content.side_e: zhè jiàn chènshān hěn hǎokàn, búguò yǒudiǎnr guì.
content.side_f: This shirt is nice, however it's a bit pricey.
content.side_g: 不过 is a softer, more conversational adversative than 但是 or 可是 — roughly 'but/however/then again.' It often hedges rather than strongly contrasts. Also functions as an adverb meaning 'merely/only' (我不过是学生). Place at the start of the second clause.

content.name: again
content.side_e: wǒ zài shìshi zhè jiàn.
content.side_f: Let me try this one again.
content.side_g: 再 marks a future repetition ('again, next time'): 再试试, 再来一次, 明天再说. Contrast with 又 (yòu), which marks past/habitual repetition (他又来了 'he came again'). 再 also means 'then/and then' in sequential actions: 先吃饭再走.

content.name: pay_money
content.side_e: wǒmen zhèr zhǐ shōu fù qián, bù shōu kǎ.
content.side_f: Here we only take cash payment, no cards.
content.side_g: 付钱 is a verb-object compound meaning 'pay money/pay cash' — separable: 付了钱, 付一点儿钱. In Chapter 9 dialogue it's used in opposition to 刷卡, so it often implies 'pay in cash' even though literally it just means 'pay.' For 'cash' specifically use 现金 (xiànjīn).

content.name: pay
content.side_e: zhè jiàn chènshān yígòng yìbǎi kuài, wǒ lái fù.
content.side_f: This shirt is 100 yuan total — I'll pay.
content.side_g: 付 is the formal 'pay' verb, taking objects like 付钱, 付款 (pay a bill), 付账 (settle the bill), 付房租 (pay rent). Compared to 给钱 (give money, colloquial), 付 sounds more transactional/formal.
